does: This paper introduces MIRAGE, a visual prompt injection attack framework that exploits vulnerabilities in multimodal large language model-based web agents. The attack works by embedding adversarial prompts within perceptually benign images generated through diffusion models, confined to spatially constrained regions like ad slots or sponsored cards that an unprivileged third party can control. The approach achieves targeted next-action hijacking—causing the MLLM-based agent to perform unintended actions—while remaining visually inconspicuous and operating under realistic threat constraints where the attacker cannot modify arbitrary content on the web platform but only controls specific legitimate UI elements.
connects: This work builds on emerging research into adversarial attacks against multimodal systems and vision-language models, extending prior work on prompt injection attacks from text-only domains to the visual domain. It addresses limitations in existing adversarial evaluation frameworks for MLLM-based agents, which typically employ visually obvious artifacts or unrealistic threat models. The paper connects to broader literature on adversarial examples in computer vision and the safety challenges of deploying large language models in interactive environments. It also relates to research on web security and the practical vulnerabilities introduced when AI agents autonomously interact with web interfaces, building on concerns about indirect attacks through UI manipulation.
enables: This work opens several important research directions for securing MLLM-based web agents against subtle visual attacks. It points toward developing robust detection mechanisms and defenses specifically designed to identify adversarially manipulated content within legitimate UI regions, potentially through improved input sanitization or anomaly detection. The research enables future work on understanding the transferability of such attacks across different MLLM architectures and on developing certified robustness guarantees for vision-language models in autonomous browsing contexts. It also sets up investigation into safer design paradigms for web agents and the development of formal frameworks for evaluating security under realistic, constrained threat models where attackers have limited but non-zero control over platform content.
